text: lay in its control over trade, particularly trade between Europe and the East, and in banking and currency manipulation. The Venetian-controlled Lombard League was born in the late twelfth century, as the Lombard League cities came to serve as centres for the usurious Lombard banking system. This Venetian-controlled alliance was in command of a financial system based on extreme usury and economic looting. In short, Venice occupies a central space within our general attempts to theorize the transition to modernity. And this is no less the case with respect to the history of gaming and gambling. Venice and the Culture of Gaming: Playing Cards To understand how pivotal a role Venice played with respect to gaming and gambling, one can start by briefly referring to one specific ludic practice, the study of which is normally confined to a very secluded group of historians and esoteric scholars: playing cards. This again of course belongs to a much larger discussion, and we cannot here go into any detail concerning the history of playing cards. While we have full and detailed knowledge of the game of chess, the same cannot be said of cards: an interesting lacuna, in and by itself, considering the pivotal role that cards would come to play in Western culture, culminating in today’s gambling craze, where almost all the games can be traced back, directly or indirectly, to the spread of cards. It is the practice of card playing that has migrated to online gaming and gambling. In fact, an argument can safely be made that from the perspective of cultural history, the introduction of cards is one of the single most important events within the Western world. It is not possible here to enter fully into the endless debates concerning the origin of playing cards. However, we know with certainty that towards the end of the fourteenth century, the practice of card playing slowly started to spread in Europe. It happened suddenly, and then spread rapidly. In his unrivalled comparative study of the tarot game, Dummett identifies 1377 as the pivotal year, where we suddenly find references to card games in Florence, Siena, Basel and Paris. The suddenness of the phenomenon leaves little doubt that cards were introduced from elsewhere in a fully developed form, and then immediately spread over a wide area of Europe (Dummett 1980: 11). The historical documents from the period explicitly talk about packs of cards as something ‘newly introduced’. Out of an apparent void, a string of references emerge pointing to the sudden arrival of playing cards in Belgium, lower Germany, Switzerland, Catalonia and across Italy. Very soon afterwards we hear of them being banned by the authorities all across Europe. Liminality and the Modern 158 Dice and certain board games were already long-established, so playing cards were a new addition to the repertoire of gambling pastimes. But more than any other game, they seemingly brought with them anti-social behaviour and cheating which immediately started to flourish in gaming houses. Violence would often break out, as huge sums were won or lost in no time. This led to bans and prohibitions as preachers demonized the game and the authorities devised ways to regulate the new craze. Most of the historical documentation we have on card playing, in fact, does not refer to its practice (we know it was widespread), but to the banning of it. Playing cards were evidently not invented in Venice, but that this was one of the first places it started to spread and become institutionalized is beyond doubt. Cards almost certainly entered Europe via Venice (Dummett 1980: 45), and there is strong evidence that they arrived to Venice via contact with the Mamluks (Dummett and Abu-Deeb 1973). This is interesting for various reasons. The Mamluks were a quite unique social formation. They were a slave-caste, but over time, and in various places, indeed, at the centres of long-established world civilizations (India, Iraq, Egypt), they developed a strong political power base as a military caste, developing proper dynasties (Waterson 2006). They existed for a thousand years, up until the nineteenth century. From the perspective of global history, the Mamluks must be recognized as a major and in many ways decisive military-political force. However, if playing cards were imports from Islamic lands, it was in Italy (and then in all of Europe) that their symbolic images were replaced by representations of courtly human beings: kings and their attendants – knights (on horseback) and foot-servants. Card playing quickly became established as an extremely prominent and ritualized activity within Italian court society, as in the courts of Ferrara, Milan and Bologna. All of this is of huge sociological relevance. The fuller discussion of this point belongs to a future study, but let us just point to the main aspects. The introduction of cards into Europe involved four fundamental changes from their original usage in the East. First, cards were introduced into Europe at a very significant point in time: with the development of paper manufacturing and the invention of printing, cards were soon to be produced for a mass market and all classes were able to play cards. In 1436 a printing press made specially for cards was imported into the Este court at Ferrara. Via the printing press, cards quickly became objects of mass consumption, whereas in Egypt and China the practice had been restricted to specific, wealthy, social classes. Second, European playing cards introduced a whole series of ‘figures’, displaying an entire symbolic universe of character traits, a marked break from Islamic cards which were beautifully ornamented, but obviously bore no images. Cards are very different from pre-existing games like dice in this respect: they play with images. Many of these images were evidently subversive, grotesque, blasphemous or otherwise provocative. Here one can mention the card known as the ‘Popesse’, one of the main figures in all early Italian tarot games.
